verification.rationale: Luther's parting saying about breaching the pope's citadel traces to D'Aubigne (fn 236, b.7 ch.11); faithful transmission of the cited source.

claims.quote: After his departure, still desirous that his firmness should not be mistaken for rebellion, Luther wrote to the emperor.
claims.claim_text: After leaving Worms, Luther wrote a letter to Charles V to clarify that his firmness should not be mistaken for rebellion.

verification.rationale: After departing, Luther wrote to Charles to clarify that his firmness was not rebellion; documented and traces to D'Aubigne b.7 ch.11.

claims.quote: “God, who is the searcher of hearts, is my witness,” he said, “that I am ready most earnestly to obey your majesty, in honor or in dishonor, in life or in death, and with no exception save the word of God, by which man lives.... For such submission in spiritual matters is a real worship, and ought to be rendered solely to the Creator.”(237)
claims.claim_text: In his letter to Charles V after leaving Worms, Luther affirmed his readiness to obey the emperor in all matters except where the word of God was concerned.

verification.rationale: Luther's letter professing readiness to obey the emperor in all but the word of God traces to D'Aubigne (fn 237, b.7 ch.11); faithful transmission.

claims.quote: On the journey from Worms, Luther’s reception was even more flattering than during his progress thither. Princely ecclesiastics welcomed the excommunicated monk, and civil rulers honored the man whom the emperor had denounced.
claims.claim_text: On his return journey from Worms, Luther was received even more favorably than on his way there, welcomed by princely ecclesiastics and civil rulers despite his excommunication.

verification.rationale: Luther's return journey drew honorable receptions from ecclesiastics and civil rulers despite his excommunication; consistent with the popular enthusiasm documented. Traces to Martyn (fn 231 context).

sources.title: The Road to Worms (enthusiastic reception on the road)

claims.quote: He was urged to preach, and notwithstanding the imperial prohibition, he again entered the pulpit.
claims.claim_text: Despite the imperial prohibition, Luther preached again during his journey home from Worms.

verification.rationale: Luther preached on the return journey despite the prohibition (e.g., at Eisenach); documented and traces to D'Aubigne/Martyn.

claims.quote: “I never pledged myself to chain up the word of God,” he said, “nor will I.”(238)
claims.claim_text: Luther said he had never pledged to chain up the word of God, nor would he.

verification.rationale: Luther's remark that he never pledged to chain the word of God traces to Martyn (fn 238); faithful transmission of the cited source.

sources.title: Martyn, Life and Times of Luther (fn 238)

claims.quote: He had not been long absent from Worms, when the papists prevailed upon the emperor to issue an edict against him.
claims.claim_text: Shortly after Luther left Worms, the papists persuaded Charles V to issue an edict against him (the Edict of Worms).

verification.rationale: Shortly after Luther left, the emperor issued the Edict of Worms against him at the urging of the papal party (Aleander drafting it). Corroborated.

sources.title: Diet of Worms — Wikipedia (Edict of Worms)

claims.quote: In this decree Luther was denounced as “Satan himself under the form of a man and dressed in a monk’s frock.”(239)
claims.claim_text: The imperial edict against Luther denounced him as "Satan himself under the form of a man and dressed in a monk's frock."

verification.rationale: The Edict's extreme language denouncing Luther (as a devil in a monk's habit) traces to Martyn (fn 239) and matches the vituperative tone of the actual edict, which Aleander drafted.

sources.title: Diet of Worms — Wikipedia (Edict's hostile characterization)

claims.quote: All persons were forbidden to harbor him, to give him food or drink, or by word or act, in public or private, to aid or abet him. He was to be seized wherever he might be, and delivered to the authorities. His adherents also were to be imprisoned, and their property confiscated. His writings were to be destroyed
claims.claim_text: The Edict of Worms forbade anyone from harboring or aiding Luther, ordered his seizure, and mandated imprisonment of his adherents, confiscation of their property, and destruction of his writings.

verification.rationale: The Edict of Worms outlawed Luther, forbade harboring or aiding him, ordered his arrest and delivery, and mandated destruction of his writings (and penalties for his adherents). Corroborated.

sources.title: Diet of Worms — Britannica (provisions of the Edict)

claims.quote: The elector of Saxony, and the princes most friendly to Luther, had left Worms soon after his departure, and the emperor’s decree received the sanction of the Diet.
claims.claim_text: Frederick, Elector of Saxony, and other princes friendly to Luther had left Worms before the Edict of Worms was issued, and the Diet gave the edict its sanction.

verification.rationale: Frederick and other Luther-friendly princes had left Worms before the Edict was formally passed (25/26 May) by a reduced Diet. Corroborated.

sources.title: Diet of Worms — Wikipedia (Edict passed after many left)

claims.quote: God gave wisdom to Frederick of Saxony to devise a plan for the Reformer’s preservation.... Upon his homeward journey, he was seized, separated from his attendants, and hurriedly conveyed through the forest to the castle of Wartburg, an isolated mountain fortress.
claims.claim_text: Frederick, Elector of Saxony, arranged for Luther to be seized on his homeward journey, separated from his attendants, and secretly taken to Wartburg Castle for protection.

verification.rationale: Frederick the Wise arranged a staged 'abduction' on Luther's homeward road, spiriting him to Wartburg Castle for protection. Corroborated.

sources.title: Luther's Time at the Wartburg (staged kidnapping by Frederick)

claims.quote: Both his seizure and his concealment were so involved in mystery that even Frederick himself for a long time knew not whither he had been conducted.
claims.claim_text: Luther's seizure and concealment at Wartburg were arranged so secretly that even Frederick himself did not know his location for a long time.

verification.rationale: The seizure was made deliberately secret; Frederick kept himself ignorant of Luther's exact whereabouts for a time so he could truthfully deny knowledge. Consistent with the standard account.

sources.title: Luther's Time at the Wartburg (companions and even Frederick kept in the dark)

claims.quote: Spring, summer, and autumn passed, and winter came, and Luther still remained a prisoner.
claims.claim_text: Luther remained in seclusion at Wartburg Castle from spring through the following winter (roughly May 1521 into early 1522).

verification.rationale: Luther remained secluded at the Wartburg from May 1521 into the following winter (until March 1522) -- through spring, summer, autumn and winter. Corroborated.

claims.quote: he cried in despair, “Alas! there is no one in this latter day of His anger, to stand like a wall before the Lord, and save Israel!”(240)
claims.claim_text: While at Wartburg, Luther cried out in despair that there was no one to "stand like a wall before the Lord, and save Israel."

verification.rationale: Luther's despairing cry ('no one to stand like a wall... and save Israel') traces to D'Aubigne (fn 240, b.9 ch.2); faithful transmission of the cited source and consistent with his documented Wartburg distress.

claims.quote: He also performed a most important service for his countrymen by translating the New Testament into the German tongue.
claims.claim_text: While in seclusion at Wartburg Castle, Luther translated the New Testament into German.

verification.rationale: At the Wartburg Luther translated the New Testament into German (the 'September Testament', published 1522). Textbook.

sources.title: Luther Bible — Wikipedia (NT translated at the Wartburg)

claims.quote: From his rocky Patmos he continued for nearly a whole year to proclaim the gospel, and rebuke the sins and errors of the times.
claims.claim_text: Luther remained at Wartburg Castle for nearly a whole year, during which he continued to write and proclaim the gospel.

verification.rationale: Luther stayed at the Wartburg about ten months (4 May 1521 – 3 March 1522), 'nearly a whole year', continuing to write and publish. Corroborated.

claims.quote: But it was not merely to preserve Luther from the wrath of his enemies, nor even to afford him a season of quiet for these important labors, that God had withdrawn His servant from the stage of public life.
claims.claim_text: God is described as having withdrawn Luther to Wartburg for a deeper providential purpose beyond mere protection or quiet for his labors.

verification.rationale: [control sample] Theological claim of a divine purpose behind Luther's withdrawal to the Wartburg beyond protection/quiet. Not empirically testable; extractor's needs_verification:false flag is correct.

claims.quote: Satan seeks to divert men’s thoughts and affections from God, and to fix them upon human agencies; he leads them to honor the mere instrument, and to ignore the Hand that directs all the events of providence.
claims.claim_text: Satan is described as seeking to divert people's honor from God to human reform leaders, a danger from which God protected the Reformation by removing Luther from public view.

verification.rationale: Theological claim about Satan diverting honor from God to human agents. Not testable.
